symptom: Breaker trips within seconds of the unit trying to start. Customer has already reset it once or twice. No cooling. Possible faint burnt-electrical smell near the disconnect or contactor.
diagnosis: Three likely root causes in order: (1) shorted compressor windings, (2) shorted wire rubbed through inside the cabinet, (3) failed start capacitor causing locked-rotor current draw.
procedure: Kill power at disconnect AND breaker. Verify both with a meter.
Inspect the contactor and all internal wiring for burn marks or melted insulation. Any melted jacket = found it, replace that wire.
Test compressor windings with a multimeter: measure ohms between C-R, C-S, R-S. Compare to nameplate values (typically C-R ~1-3Ω, R-S ~2-5Ω, C-R + R-S should equal C-S within 5%).
Test winding-to-ground: each terminal to compressor shell should read open (infinite). Any continuity = shorted to ground = dead compressor.
If compressor passes: swap the start capacitor and retry. If breaker holds, old cap was the cause.
If compressor is shorted: quote replacement (3-5 hours, refrigerant recovery required) or recommend full system if unit is >10 years old.
NEVER reset the breaker more than twice. Repeated tripping welds contactor points and can start a fire.
safety_notes: Tripping breakers signals a short circuit — there is live fault current somewhere. Assume any exposed conductor is energized until proven otherwise.
Burnt-smell = check for fire hazard before continuing. Pull the disconnect and inspect.
240V single-phase to ground is lethal. Hand-in-pocket rule when working near live terminals.
